comments.text: Building on Ho Boon Suan's note for the squarefree subquestion (c), we are able to show: for the sums-of-two-squares set $E$, and every $0 < c < 1/10$ and all sufficiently large $n$, every positive integer $t$ satisfying $a \in E \Longleftrightarrow a + t \in E$ for $1 \leq a \leq n$ must exceed $\exp(n^c)$. We don't settle whether $E$ has the translation property; this just bounds the size of any hypothetical local period. Writeup at https://leon2k2k2k.github.io/erdos675a.pdf The strategy is the same shape as Boon's. If $p \equiv 3 \pmod 4$ and $p \nmid t$, then by Dirichlet on modulus $4p^2$ one finds two primes $\ell_0, \ell_1 \equiv 1 \pmod 4$ (hence in $E$) that are both $\equiv -t \pmod p$ but differ $\pmod {p^2}$, contradicting the mod-$p^2$ lifting that the translation property forces inside $E$. This implies that every small $p \equiv 3 \pmod 4$ divides $t$, and the prime number theorem in arithmetic progressions gives the bound. The exponent $1/(2L) = 1/10$ comes from Xylouris's $L \leq 5$, where $L$ is Linnik's constant. Discovered with the assistance of OpenAI's Codex inside the Rethlas pipeline; verified by hand and not yet formalized.

About the last question on squarefree numbers, I believe I have found, with the assistance of GPT-5.4 Pro, a proof that $t_n>\exp(n^c)$ for sufficiently large $n$, for every $0<c<25/72$; see https://boonsuan.github.io/erdos675_squarefree.pdf. (I've read the proof and I think it's correct; I have not formalized it.) The main idea is as follows: Suppose a translate $t$ preserves squarefreeness on $1,\dots,n$. Then we show that $p^2\mid t$ for every prime $p\le n^c$ by assuming that $p^2\not\mid t$ and using a bound on the least squarefree integer in a reduced residue class to contradict the translation property. The statement of the squarefree bound (due to Nunes) used is as follows: Given a modulus $q\ge1$ and a reduced residue class $r\bmod q$, let $L(q,r)$ be the least positive squarefree integer $m$ such that $m\equiv r\pmod q$. For every $\varepsilon>0$, there is a constant $C_\varepsilon$ such that, uniformly for all $q\ge1$ and all $r$ with $(r,q)=1$, we have $L(q,r)\le C_\varepsilon q^{36/25+\varepsilon}$.
Congrats! I ran standard check which found no issues.
Looks good to me! By the way, I greatly appreciate your providing of a human summary of the argument in the comment that highlights the key ideas; this makes it significantly easier to evaluate.

Building on ho boon suan’s squarefree-period argument, the exponent $25/72$ can be improved using the recent Zhong--Zhang result on squarefree integers in arithmetic progressions to prime-power moduli. Let $\beta>3/4$ be an admissible exponent for squarefree distribution modulo $q=p^2$. Then the least squarefree integer in any reduced class modulo $p^2$ is\[ O\!\left(p^{2/\beta+\varepsilon}\right). \]Re-running the divisibility argument gives\[ p^2 \mid t_n \]for all primes $p \le n^c$, provided\[ c < \min(\beta/2,\,25/61). \]The second number $25/61$ comes from the case $p \mid t_n$, $p^2 \nmid t_n$, using Nunes modulo $p$: choose squarefree $b \equiv -t_n/p \pmod p$, then\[ pb \le p^{61/25+\varepsilon}. \]Since Zhong--Zhang break the $3/4$-barrier for prime-power moduli, this gives at least\[ t_n > \exp(n^c)\qquad (c < 3/8), \]improving the previously posted $c < 25/72$. Extracting the exact $\beta$ from their Corollary~1.2 may give a slightly stronger exponent. (suggested improvement is by gpt 5.5 pro)

I have a question about this: Zhong-Zhang Theorem 1.1 requires $q = p^n$ with $n >= 4$ and Corollary 1.2 requires $n >= 5$, but ho boon suan's argument needs it for $q = p^2$, so $n = 2$. Am I missing something? Also it is strange that I wasn't able to the pre-print on arxiv.
The description should say $A=\{ n:b\not\mid n \text{ for all } b\in B\}$. Currently it has $A$ in place of $B$.
